::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility is Determined For most felonies, the sentencing court must impose a maximum sentence according to the offense grade (5, 10, 20, or life) with no discretion to determine the minimum. Minimum terms are set by the parole board except when a mandatory minimum statute applies.
## In Prison Past Their Parole Eligibility In 2020, there were people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up percent of people* in prison. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: From 2016 to 2017, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility stayed the same. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::

## State Notes "What if we change the section from Missing Data to more of a high-level state-specific Methods section? It can include: 1) Total N for the state 2) How we determined parole eligibility (e.g., what formulas did we use to estimate parole eligibility year) 3) Missing data patterns" - Jess S.


# Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 31 percent, changing from 5,912 in 2010 to 4,102 in 2022. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2022, most people in prison were Other race(s), non-Hispanic people, representing 63 percent of people in prison. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Sex In 2022, most people in prison were males, representing 89 percent of people in prison. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, most people in prison were between 25 to 34 years old, representing 30 percent of people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, most people in prison were incarcerated for property, representing 27 percent of people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Sentence Lengths In 2020, most people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 58 percent of people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::


# Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, prison releases decreased 42 percent, dropping from 1,882 in 2010 to 1,098 in 2020. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
In 2020, 26 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 26 percent increase compared to 2015. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, most people released from prison were Other race(s), non-Hispanic people, representing 67 percent of prison releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Sex In 2020, most people released from prison were males, representing 86 percent of prison releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, most releases from prison were people between 35 to 44 years old, representing 33 percent of prison releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, most people released from prison were incarcerated for property offenses, representing 41 percent of people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
# Disparities ## Disparities in Incarceration ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/rri_infographic_black_Hawaii.png){width=1150} ::: ::: Black people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 3 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Hawaii. ::: {.cell} ::: ### Gender
:::
## Time Served ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: On average, Black people spent 1.1 more years in prison and Hispanic people spent 0.1 more years in prison compared to White people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: For murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, people of Other race(s) people spent 11.6 more years in prison on average compared to Hispanic people, who had the shortest time served for these offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Gender On average, females spent 2 fewer years in prison compared to males in Hawaii. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: For other violent offenses offenses, males spent 3.3 more years in prison on average compared to females. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Disparities in Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity ### Gender :::
## Years in Prison After Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity Black people spent an average of 1 more years in prison past parole eligibility and Hispanic people spent an average of 2.6 more years in prison past parole eligibility compared to White people. ```{=html}

## How Disparities Are Calculated While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material. State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals. *States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.*

:::
